name: Canarina
classification_notes: Canarina is a display typeface drawing on the visual culture of the Canary Islands, with wide angles and expressive letterforms inspired by the plastic arts of Eduardo Millares Sall and César Manrique rather than typographic convention.
summary: A display typeface rooted in the visual arts of the Canary Islands, drawing on the plastic works of Eduardo Millares Sall and César Manrique for its wide, expressive letterforms.
description: Designed by Ariel Martín Pérez and released under the SIL Open Font License, Canarina takes its name from the Canarina canariensis plant native to the Canary Islands. In the absence of a strong local typographic tradition, the design turns to 20th-century graphic and sculptural arts of the archipelago, finding its distinctive wide angles and vernacular character in informalist painting and abstract sculpture. Version 2.0 added new weights, improved Latin language support and refined drawing and spacing throughout.
foundry_blurb: Tunera describe Canarina as a font inspired by the Canary Islands, aiming to recreate traces of their history and character. Named after the Canarina canariensis plant, the design draws not from local metal type traditions but from the visual arts of figures such as Eduardo Millares Sall and César Manrique, whose plastic works are reflected in the typeface's wide angles and strong vernacular look.

name: Ampoule
classification_notes: Ampoule is a modular display typeface built entirely from three geometric components arranged on a strict grid, producing a pixel-like, tubular aesthetic reminiscent of LED information boards and neon signage.
summary: A strictly modular display family built from just three components, conceived as a tribute to Cyberpunk 2077 and the aesthetic of transit information boards.
description: Drawn from only three geometric primitives — a dot, a double-height rounded rectangle and a triple-height rounded rectangle — the entire typeface derives from a single invariable grid unit. Styles include Neon and Rats with slanted, backslanted and Tube variants, covering 748 glyphs with Latin European language support, Vietnamese, thematic icons and gender-inclusive French ligatures. Counterintuitively, the design reads more clearly at small sizes than large, a consequence of its rigorous minimalist construction.
foundry_blurb: Tunera describe Ampoule as above all a display family, the name meaning 'light bulb' in French, with an appearance evoking neon lamps. Originally imagined as a tribute to Cyberpunk 2077, designer Ando conceived it as a typeface that might have appeared on the information boards of Night City's NCART metro. Built on a strict matrix of three components in three sizes, the family also functions as an exercise in readability minimalism, and extends into a contextualising dystopian world-building concept with 3D renders by Carla Manckoundia.

name: Luperca
classification_notes: Based on Roman square capitals with epigraphic Latin shapes, calligraphic details and chisel-inspired serifs, Luperca sits firmly in the display category as an inscriptional or lapidary revival style.
summary: An all-caps display typeface drawing on Roman square capitals, epigraphic ligatures and chisel-engraved serifs, released in five optical sizes for editorial titling.
description: Designed by Ariel Martín Pérez for the mythology-themed 11th issue of Cercle magazine, Luperca adapts epigraphic Latin conventions — including ligatures and letters that overshoot the cap height or baseline to create imbrications — into a digital format suited to compact titling. The letterforms blend sources from different periods: the tail of the Q references the Lyon Tablet, serif construction follows chiselled stone-cutting, while the bar of the E reflects a calligraphic pen rotation. Five optical display sizes are included, all under the SIL Open Font License.
foundry_blurb: Tunera describe Luperca as an exploration of a possible evolution of Roman square capitals, originally designed for an editorial context dedicated to mythology. The typeface collects epigraphic Latin shapes such as ligatures and letters that exceed standard cap height or baseline to produce imbrications, rendering them in digital form. It balances calligraphic and typographic structures drawn from multiple historical periods, and is available in five optical display sizes under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Azabache
classification_notes: Azabache defies conventional serif or sans categorisation; its oval modular construction, reversed contrast direction and heavy texture place it firmly in experimental display territory, distinct from traditional calligraphic or digital type.
summary: A modular display typeface built from oval forms rather than vector paths, with reversed stroke contrast and a heavy, textured result.
description: Designed by Ariel Martín Pérez using a trackpad in Processing before letter shapes were reconstructed from oval modules in Glyphs, the method is deliberately iterative and resists conventional type-drawing workflows. Its contrast direction runs counter to Western calligraphic tradition, and the result sits between the handwritten and the digital in a way the designer describes as cyborg. Available under the SIL Open Font License as a free download from Tunera.
foundry_blurb: Tunera describe Azabache as the outcome of a research project by Ariel Martín Pérez exploring alternative methods for designing digital letters without conventional vector path drawing. The letterforms were first sketched by hand with a trackpad on Processing, then reproduced using oval modules in Glyphs, yielding a heavily textured typeface with traces of handwriting, a streamlined digital quality, and a contrast direction that inverts the norms of Western calligraphy.

name: Choso
classification_notes: Choso is a symbol and ornament typeface rather than a text face, built from geometric decorative figures drawn from the frieze patterns of Canary Islands house facades. Its purpose is purely decorative and pictographic, placing it firmly in the display/ornamental category.
summary: A symbol font collecting geometric decorative motifs from the frieze sections of 20th-century Canary Islands house facades.
description: Drawn from the upper decorative friezes of popular single-storey houses across the Canarian archipelago, the glyphs in Choso are geometric symbols gathered over a long period from multiple islands. They can be used in isolation or as repeating patterns, reflecting the two modes in which they appear on the original buildings. Released under the SIL Open Font License, it is a collaborative project by Ariel Martín Pérez intended to grow with new symbols over time.
foundry_blurb: Tunera describe Choso as a typeface inspired by the geometric symbols found in the frieze sections of many 20th-century Canary Islands house facades, particularly on single-storey popular houses known as 'casas terreras'. The name means 'house' or 'home' in the Canary Islands dialect. It is a collaborative project designed to be extended with new symbols in future releases, and is available free under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Tanklager
classification_notes: Described as a mixture of DIN and grotesk features with irregular optical corrections and geometric diacritics, derived from a hand-painted sign; its intentionally rough, vernacular character places it firmly in the display category.
summary: A two-width display grotesque drawn from a hand-painted oil tank sign in Switzerland, mixing DIN geometry with deliberate optical imperfection.
description: Based on a vernacular hand-painted sign spotted on an oil tank in Visp, Switzerland, this typeface blends DIN and grotesk structures with uneven optical corrections that are kept as features rather than fixed. Capital and lowercase letters sometimes follow different structural logic, and the geometric diacritics are treated with unusual freedom. It comes in two widths: Original, which preserves proportions close to the source sign, and Kompakt, suited to headlines and tighter settings.
foundry_blurb: Tunera describe Tanklager as inspired by a strange hand-painted sign on the side of an oil tank in Visp, Switzerland. It debuted in the 9th issue of Jef Klak, a review dedicated to ingestion and digestion. The design mixes DIN and grotesk features, with capital and lowercase letters sometimes following different structures, deliberately recycled shapes, and geometric diacritics. It comes in two cuts: Original and Kompakt.

name: Lobular
classification_notes: Lobular is a display typeface with organic, bulbous letterforms inspired by biological cross-sections and graffiti bubble letters, with flat inktraps used as structural drawing tools rather than functional print aids.
summary: An experimental display typeface by Ariel Martín Pérez built around bulbous, biomorphic letterforms that evoke kidney cross-sections and graffiti bubble letters.
description: Designed by Ariel Martín Pérez and released under the SIL Open Font License, Lobular draws its forms from biological cross-sections and urban bubble lettering, creating dense dark masses on the page where light is caught inside the counters. Flat inktraps serve a purely structural role, defining the counters rather than compensating for ink spread in print. The design is the product of resolving topological constraints, keeping curves and gaps consistent, and at times using those constraints as a prompt for experimental letterforms.
foundry_blurb: Tunera describe Lobular as a typeface resembling cross-sections of kidneys or the transversal section of an intestinal tract, creating dark masses on the page's surface in which light is ensnared. Its entirely flat inktraps serve a structural rather than practical role, and the design also recalls bubble letters from graffiti traditions worldwide. Designing it was framed as a matter of solving topological problems, with those constraints also serving as inspiration for experimental letterforms.

name: Amakan
classification_notes: A monospaced display typeface built on a square grid, with letterforms derived from the geometric weave patterns of split-bamboo mats rather than conventional calligraphic or typographic traditions.
summary: A Filipino typeface by Jad Maza that translates the woven diagonal and diamond motifs of split-bamboo amakan mats into three distinct typographic styles on a square grid.
description: Designed by Jad Maza of Iloilo, Philippines, the typeface began as a 36 Days of Type entry in 2022 and has since grown into a functional font of 66 glyphs per style. The three styles, Sheet, Strips and Stripes, each interpret the same letterforms differently: Sheet fills the full glyph area, Strips constructs characters from horizontal strands and Stripes fills negative space with vertical lines. Contextual alternates cycle the weave direction, while a reverse stylistic set and blank-space set extend its compositional possibilities.
foundry_blurb: Tunera describe Amakan as a nod to local materials and craftsmanship, patterned after the woven split-bamboo mats used as wall cladding for traditional nipà huts in the Philippines. The typeface is constructed on a square grid to produce its monospaced format, and includes basic Latin, numerals, Filipino diacritics and some punctuation across three styles: Sheet, Strips and Stripes. Released under the SIL Open Font License, it is free to use, distribute and modify with credit to Tunera and the designer.

name: Nyght Serif
classification_notes: Rooted in study of Caslon but substantially transformed by calligraphic influence and modern serif conventions, featuring contrasting smooth and sharp curves with blade serifs and spurs.
summary: A contemporary serif by Maksym Kobuzan that pairs smooth curves with sharp blade serifs and spurs, winner of a Type Directors Club Typographic Excellence Certificate in 2023.
description: Designed by Kyiv-based Maksym Kobuzan, the typeface began as a study of Caslon but diverged substantially under the influence of calligraphy and modern serif design, resulting in a distinctive tension between fluid curves and cutting edges. Three weights from Regular to Bold are available with corresponding italics, covering an extended Latin character set plus Ukrainian and Bulgarian Cyrillic. Published under the SIL Open Font License whilst still in active development, with further weights and refinements planned.
foundry_blurb: Tunera describe Nyght Serif as a contemporary serif with a spicy character, combining smooth and sharp curves in the form of blade serifs and spurs. Though its origins lie in study of William Caslon's typefaces, calligraphic and modern serif influences transformed the design considerably. The typeface received a Typographic Excellence Certificate at the 69th Type Directors Club competition in 2023 and is still in active development, with updates ongoing.

name: Piscolabis
classification_notes: A bold condensed display face derived from hand-painted European signage, making it squarely a display typeface with vernacular, sign-painting roots rather than a conventional grotesque or geometric sans.
summary: A bold, condensed display typeface drawn from hand-painted European signage, notable for its high ascenders and restrained character.
description: Developed as a sans-serif offshoot of Ariel Martín Pérez's Picaflor, Piscolabis shares its parent's x-height but quickly grew its own identity. Its forms are rooted in hand-painted shop signs found across Europe, giving headlines a vernacular warmth without tipping into novelty. The high ascenders are the most distinctive structural feature, lending titles a confident vertical stretch suited to editorial and poster work.
foundry_blurb: Tunera describe Piscolabis as a bold condensed display typeface inspired by hand-painted signs found across Europe. The project began as a sans-serif spin-off of Picaflor, sharing the same x-height, but evolved its own personality. High ascenders are cited as a defining feature, and the name itself references a type of Spanish bar known for serving snacks, rooted in the designer's childhood memories.

name: Pescante
classification_notes: A curveless, flat-brush-inspired geometric display typeface with unusual cursive letter shapes and angled dents; its distinctive construction places it firmly in the display category rather than a conventional geometric sans.
summary: A curveless display typeface by Ariel Martín Pérez, derived from painted warehouse lettering found on the island of La Gomera in the Canary Islands.
description: Drawn without curves, Pescante takes its visual cues from letters painted with a flat brush, referencing a piece of lettering on an old warehouse facade in La Gomera. Unusual details appear alongside the geometric rigour: cursive letter forms and dents at inner angles give the design a handmade irregularity that flat geometry alone would not produce. The wide stems and even rhythm are described by the designer as an echo of the davit cranes, decommissioned cargo-loading structures that once served La Gomera's steep, portless coastline.
foundry_blurb: Pescante is a curveless typeface inspired by lettering found on the front of an old warehouse in La Gomera, Canary Islands. Its design references geometric letters painted with a flat brush, with unusual additions including cursive letter shapes and dents in the inner angles. The name refers to the davit cranes used to load cargo on boats where the island's abrupt coastline made a port impossible, and the typeface's wide stems and regular rhythm recall the pillars of those decommissioned structures. Version 1.0 covers all Latin-based European languages and some symbols, released under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Roubaix Industrielle
classification_notes: A fully modular, all-caps display typeface built from brick-shaped units, with optical size variants that alter internal texture. Its constructivist, geometric character places it firmly in display territory.
summary: A fully modular, all-caps typeface built from brick-shaped units, commissioned by the Roubaix Tourism Office as a tribute to the city's industrial and textile heritage.
description: Designed by Ariel Martín Pérez for the Roubaix Tourism Office and atelier Bien Vu, the typeface draws its letterform logic from the brick construction prevalent in the northern French city. Five optical sizes shift the internal texture of each glyph, with smaller sizes gaining connective seam-like details that reference the textile industry alongside the architecture. The family includes a Regular version suitable for screen and print, plus several variants with differing letter heights for print use, all available as a variable font spanning the optical size range.
foundry_blurb: Tunera describes Roubaix Industrielle as a typeface inspired by the history of Roubaix, a northern French city that grew during the 19th-century industrial revolution. Commissioned by the Roubaix Tourism Office and atelier Bien Vu under the name 'I love Roubaix', its design is based on brick-shaped modules referencing the city's predominant building method, while internal connections at smaller optical sizes evoke textile seam patterns, bridging the city's twin identities of brick architecture and textile industry.

name: Picaflor
classification_notes: Described as a hybrid display typeface blending French Didone construction with Spanish vernacular exuberance and calligraphic details, placing it firmly in the display category with a Didone subcategory.
summary: An all-caps display typeface mixing French Didone structure with Spanish vernacular calligraphic detail, originally drawn for a flower-themed magazine issue.
description: Designed by Ariel Martín Pérez for the ninth issue of Cercle magazine, a publication dedicated to flowers, Picaflor merges the high-contrast vertical stress of French Didone letterforms with the exuberant calligraphic energy of Spanish vernacular lettering. The family is all-capitals and includes optical sizes as well as a variable version. Released under the SIL Open Font License and free to use, distribute and modify with credit.
foundry_blurb: Tunera describe Picaflor as a hybrid display typeface with calligraphic details, characterising it as French Didone meeting Spanish vernacular exuberance. It was created specifically for the ninth issue of Cercle magazine, which was dedicated to flowers, and its first public release includes an all-capitals character set, optical sizes and a variable version.

name: Teranoptia
classification_notes: Teranoptia contains no letterforms whatsoever; each keystroke produces an illustrated creature, plant or ornamental motif drawn from medieval and Bayeux Tapestry sources. It functions as a dingbat or pictorial font, best classified as a display ornament face.
summary: A letterless pictorial font by Ariel Martín Pérez that maps keyboard characters to hand-drawn chimeric creatures and plants inspired by the Bayeux Tapestry and medieval marginalia.
description: Rather than letterforms, each key produces an illustrated animal, plant or hybrid creature drawn from medieval manuscript traditions and the Bayeux Tapestry. The current release covers A–Z with some diacritics and symbols, giving enough coverage to build continuous border ornaments or scatter marginalia through a layout. Released under the SIL Open Font License and available as a free download from Tunera.
foundry_blurb: Tunera describe Teranoptia as a typeface without letters, a peculiar contraption that lets you imagine chimeric creatures simply by typing on a keyboard. Inspired by the Bayeux Tapestry, medieval illustrations and children's books, it can be used to create border ornaments, daydream about monsters or add marginalia to layouts. The first version covers A–Z with some diacritics and symbols representing plants and animals, with further elements planned for future releases.

name: Isenheim
classification_notes: Described as a serif family with medieval influences, originally conceived as a revival from a type found in a book on the Isenheim Altarpiece, with lombardic influences and MUFI rarities pointing firmly to a medieval or blackletter-adjacent serif classification.
summary: A serif revival by Benoît Ferran drawing on medieval type found in a Payot Lausanne publication on the Isenheim Altarpiece, extended with lombardic influences and MUFI rarities.
description: Originally conceived from a typeface found in a small illustrated book on Matthias Grünewald's Isenheim Altarpiece, the design has since evolved well beyond that source into a standalone family. It includes a substantial set of alternates, ligatures and rare MUFI characters alongside lombardic influences, giving it a richly historical character. Released under the SIL Open Font License and available as a free download from Tunera.
foundry_blurb: Tunera describe Isenheim as a serif family with medieval influences, packed with alternates and ligatures including MUFI rarities and lombardic touches. Régulier began as a revival from type found in a Payot Lausanne Orbis Pictus book on the Isenheim Altarpiece, but evolved into something independent, as though the altarpiece itself gradually merged with the type.

name: Malebolge
classification_notes: Described as extravagant with letter forms inspired by the ditches of Dante's eighth circle of hell, suggesting highly irregular, expressive forms that place it firmly in the display/experimental category rather than any conventional classification.
summary: A deliberately extravagant display typeface by Ariel Martín Pérez, named after the eighth circle of Dante's Inferno and shaped by the anxieties of 2020.
description: The letterforms draw their character from the ditches dividing Dante's Malebolge, with jagged or fractured edges running through the design. Released under the SIL Open Font License, this first public version offers a limited glyphset with further characters planned. The typeface is free to use, distribute and modify with credit to Tunera and the designer.
foundry_blurb: Tunera describe Malebolge as a typeface named after the eighth circle of Dante's hell, reserved for minor fraudsters and divided into ditches that informed the letter edges. The design was inspired by the dark atmosphere of 2020, encompassing the global pandemic, despotism and rising inequality. Version 0.7, subtitled Adversarial, is the first public release offering extravagant letters in a limited glyphset, with more to follow.

name: Paysage
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist-style sans-serif, with influences from Antique Olive, Rotis Sans and Verdana — all humanist or humanist-adjacent designs. The distinctive double-storey 'g' and regular rhythm further confirm the humanist classification.
summary: A humanist sans-serif by Anton Moglia, drawing on Antique Olive, Rotis Sans and Verdana to produce a quiet, text-friendly typeface in four weights.
description: Originally begun in 2016 as Garcia Regular, the typeface was redesigned, extended and renamed Paysage in 2020. Its four weights give enough range for typographic hierarchy, while the design's regular rhythm and distinctive double-storey 'g' make it suitable for running text, captions and annotations alike. Latin-script coverage spans most European languages, and the font is distributed under the SIL Open Font Licence.
foundry_blurb: Tunera describe Paysage as a humanist-style sans-serif characterised by a distinctive 'g' and regular rhythm, designed to give a feeling of simplicity and tranquility. Inspired by Roger Excoffon's Antique Olive, Otl Aicher's Rotis Sans and Matthew Carter's Verdana, it is suited to short or long texts, legends and annotations. Version 1.0, released in 2020, is a redesign and extension of Garcia Regular, offering four weights for greater versatility.

name: Manosque
classification_notes: Manosque is drawn with a flat brush to harmonise letterforms sourced from late 19th and early 20th century French public signage, placing it firmly in the display category with a vernacular brush lettering subcategory.
summary: A flat-brush display typeface drawn from early 20th century French public signage, inspired by lettering found at the Manosque train station.
description: Rooted in the hand-lettered signage of French public transport and urban infrastructure from the late 19th and early 20th centuries, this typeface uses flat-brush drawing as a method to unify shapes pulled from varied historical sources. The result is a display face with the informal warmth of vernacular lettering, kept coherent by the discipline of a single tool. Released under the SIL Open Font License and available as a free download.
foundry_blurb: Manosque takes its name from a city in the south of France, inspired by lettering found at its train station as well as other public signs and transport lettering from the late 19th and early 20th centuries. The designer used a flat brush to draw the forms, finding it an effective way to bring harmony to shapes gathered from multiple historical sources. The typeface supports all Latin-based European languages.

name: Brassia
classification_notes: Brassia's freestyle letterforms draw on art nouveau aesthetics via the Solarpunk movement, with pen-derived translation contrast and vernacular influences from Brussels signage. Its expressive, decorative character places it firmly in the display category.
summary: A capitals-only display face rooted in Solarpunk aesthetics and Brussels vernacular lettering, with pen-derived contrast and a deliberately ancient-yet-futuristic character.
description: Designed initially as a teaching tool for vector tracing at a 2019 Velvetyne workshop in Liège, Brassia draws on the art nouveau leanings of the Solarpunk science fiction genre. Its letterforms take a freestyle approach to the Latin alphabet, with translation contrast typical of pen writing and some characters derived from ancient and vernacular signs found in Brussels. The current version is capitals-only, with alternative forms tucked into the lowercase slots; the glyph set may expand depending on demand.
foundry_blurb: Tunera describe Brassia as a typeface originally prototyped during a Velvetyne Type Foundry workshop for the Fig. festival in Liège in February 2019. Its design is inspired by Solarpunk, a science fiction genre oriented towards clean energy futures with an art nouveau aesthetic, and the letters were drawn to feel both ancient and futuristic, informed in part by vernacular signs found in Brussels.

name: Kobata
classification_notes: Built entirely from rectangles and circles on a strict grid, Kobata is a rigorously geometric experimental display face with no conventional typographic construction; its rule-based structure and visual interference effects place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A rule-based experimental face built solely from rectangles and circles, producing a geometric rhythm with glitch-like visual interference.
description: Designed by Ariel Martín Pérez and released under the SIL Open Font License, Kobata restricts its construction to two shapes: rectangles, which may only meet at corners, and circles, the sole shape permitted optical correction beyond the 50×50 UPM grid. The result draws loose references from calligraphy, embroidery and pixel art, with vertical stroke repetition generating a visual interference akin to a digital glitch, while circular counters read as dark spots on the page. A bold style and variable version were added in March 2020, with the bold pushing further from the grid to introduce new contrast variation.
foundry_blurb: Tunera describe Kobata as an experimental typeface governed by three simple rules limiting forms to rectangles and circles, with rectangles meeting only at corners and circles being the only shapes permitted optical correction. They note it takes hints from calligraphy, embroidery and digital art, and suggest it for short texts, visual identity and art projects where a dramatic effect is desired. A bold and variable version are available, both free to use, distribute and modify under the SIL Open Font License.
